text:
1.

Guesthouse for Gentlemen

The view is obscured by clouds of steam from the locomotive, now drifting across the platform. We must peer out from beneath them to see everything, let ourselves be blinded for a moment by the grey mist, until the sight that emerges from the ordeal is sharp, piercing, all-seeing.

Then we shall see the slabs of the platform, squares overgrown with blades of frail little plants, a space that wants at all costs to preserve order and symmetry.

In a moment a left shoe appears upon them, brown, leather, no longer new, and at once a second joins it, the right one; this one seems even more the worse for wear, its toe a little scuffed, the surface in several small places revealing paler specks. For a moment the shoes stand there irresolute, but then the left one moves forward. For an instant that movement reveals a black cotton sock beneath the trouser leg. The black recurs in the open skirts of a frieze overcoat; the day is warm. A brown leather suitcase is held by a small hand, pale and bloodless; under its weight the veins have tensed and now point back to their sources somewhere deep in the sleeve. Beneath the coat there flashes a flannel jacket of rather poor quality, and somewhat crumpled besides by the long journey. Tiny pale specks of some indeterminate dirt are visible on it, the scales of the world. The white collar of the shirt, one of those detachable ones, has evidently been changed quite recently, for its whiteness is fresher than the whiteness of the shirt itself, and it contrasts with the earthy colour of the traveller's face. Light eyes, brows and lashes make that face seem unhealthy. The whole figure against the intensely red sky of the west gives the disquieting impression of having come here, to these melancholy mountains, from the afterlife.

The traveller walks with the other arrivals toward the main hall of a station surprisingly large for this mountain region; what sets him apart from them is that he walks unhurriedly, even dawdling a little, and that no one greets him, no one has come to meet him. He sets his suitcase down on the worn tiled floor and pulls lined gloves onto his hands. One of them, the right, the hand rolled into a tube, travels after a moment to his mouth to receive a parade of short, dry coughs.

The young man hunches over and searches his pocket for a handkerchief. For a moment his fingers touch the place where, in the pocket beneath the cloth of the coat, his passport is concealed. If we focus our attention for a moment, we shall see the fanciful handwriting of some Galician official who has carefully filled in the document's boxes: Mieczysław Wojnicz, Catholic, student at the Lwów Polytechnic, born 1889, eyes blue, medium height, face long, hair fair.

And now Wojnicz crosses the main concourse of the station in Dittersbach, not far from Waldenburg, walking uncertainly through the gloomy, lofty hall, where echo presumably lives up on the highest cornices, and he feels someone’s eyes carefully inspecting him from behind the ticket windows in the waiting room. He checks the time on the great clock: late, the last train from Breslau. He hesitates for a moment, then goes out in front of the station building, only to be at once enfolded in the broad arms of the uneven, jagged line of the mountain horizon.

It is mid-September, yet here, as the traveller notices with surprise, summer is long gone and the first fallen leaves already lie on the ground. The last few days must have been rainy, for a light mist still fills the landscape almost completely, sparing only the dark lines of the streams. He can feel in his lungs that he is high up; that is good for his disease-weary body. Wojnicz stands on the station steps, suspiciously examining his shoes with their thin leather soles; he will have to think about winter boots. In Lwów the asters and zinnias were still in bloom, and no one was thinking of autumn at all. Here, by contrast, the high horizon makes it darker, and the colours seem brighter, almost vulgar. At this moment he is overtaken by a melancholy he knows well, the kind proper to people convinced of their own imminent death. He feels that the world around him is scenery painted on a paper screen, that he could stick a finger into that monumental landscape and bore a hole in it leading straight into nothingness. And it, that nothingness, will begin to pour out of there like a flood and will in the end reach him too, seize him by the throat. He must shake his head to rid himself of the image. The image shatters into droplets and falls onto the leaves. Fortunately, an ungainly vehicle resembling a light carriage comes rolling toward him along the road. Sitting in it is a skinny, freckled boy in a strange outfit. He is wearing something like a military jacket of hard-to-place provenance, for it resembles neither a Prussian uniform, which here would have been understandable, nor any other, and a military forage cap tilted rakishly on his head. Without a word he stops in front of Wojnicz and, muttering something under his breath, takes his luggage.

“How are you, good man?” Wojnicz asks politely in his schoolbook German, but he waits in vain for an answer; the boy pulls his cap down over his eyes and impatiently points to a place for him in the carriage.

And at once they set off. First through the little town over the cobblestones, then by a road that, in the falling dark, leads them along a winding track between the steep mountain slopes and through the forest. The murmur of a nearby stream accompanies them without cease, and its smell, which always so unsettles Wojnicz: the smell of damp undergrowth, rotting leaves, stones forever wet, of water. He tries to ask the driver some question that might establish contact, for instance how much longer they will be driving, how he recognized him at the station, what his name is, but the man does not so much as look back and says nothing. The gas lamp mounted to the boy's right half lights his face, which in profile resembles the snout of a mountain rodent, a marmot, and Wojnicz surmises that the driver must be either deaf or brazenly rude.

At last, after some three quarters of an hour, they emerge from the shadow of the forest and drive into a surprisingly flat valley, an unexpected plateau here among the wooded mountains. The sky is fading, but that piercing high horizon is still visible, the one that seems to every newcomer from the lowlands to come right up to the throat.

"Görbersdorf," the driver says suddenly, in a boyish voice, unexpectedly high.

But Wojnicz sees nothing, nothing but the dense wall of darkness peeling away in whole sheets from the mountain slopes unceremoniously. Only when his eyes grow used to it does a viaduct suddenly emerge before them, beneath which they enter the village, and beyond it the enormous bulk of a red-brick building, and at once other, smaller buildings, and a street, and even two gas lamps. The brick building turns out to be a colossus, it grows before his eyes, and the movement of the vehicle draws rows of lit windows out of the darkness. Their light is a dirty yellow. Wojnicz cannot take his eyes off this unexpected triumphant sight and keeps looking back at it for a long time, until it sinks into the darkness like an enormous steamship.

The trap now turns into a narrow side lane along the stream, passes over a small bridge where the wheels set up a clatter like gunfire. At last it stops before a sizeable wooden building of a most peculiar architecture, which brings to mind a house made of matchsticks -- so many verandas, little balconies, and terraces. A pleasant light burns in the windows. Beneath the first-floor windows stands a beautiful inscription in Fraktur, cut from thick sheet metal:

Gästehaus für Herren

Wojnicz gets down from the trap with relief and draws into his lungs a mighty draught of this new air, which people say cures the gravest conditions. Yet perhaps he does so too soon, for suddenly a fit of coughing assails him so violently that he has to brace himself against the railing of the little bridge. And then, coughing, he feels the cold and the unpleasant slickness of the rotten wood, and the good first impression bursts. He cannot stop the violent spasms of his diaphragm, and an overpowering fear engulfs him: that in a moment he will suffocate, that this attack will be the last. He tries to drive the panic away, just as Doctor Sokołowski advised him, to think of a meadow full of flowers, of warm sunshine. He tries very hard, though his eyes are watering and his face is flushed with blood. It seems to him that in a moment he will cough up his soul.

But then he feels a grip on his shoulder, and a tall, well-built, grizzled man holds out his hand to him. Through his tears Wojnicz catches sight of his pink, healthy face.

“Now, now, my dear sir. Let us pull ourselves together,” says the other with such assurance and such a broad smile that the guest, barely alive from coughing, feels an urge to press close to him and let himself be led to bed like a child. Oh yes, exactly. Child. Bed. With a certain embarrassment he throws his arms around the man’s neck and lets himself be led through the hall fragrant with spruce smoke and up the soft, carpet-padded stairs. All this summons some distant association with wrestling, with a manly sport in which hard bodies press against one another, rub against one another, strike one another, though not to do one another harm but, quite the contrary, to show one another tenderness and attachment under the pretext of combat. He yields to the strong hands, allows himself to be led to a room upstairs, seated on the bed, and stripped of coat and sweater.

Wilhelm Opitz, for that is how the man introduces himself, pointing to his chest with a finger, covers him with a woollen blanket and, from hands that appear for a moment in the crack of the door, takes a mug of hot, savoury broth. As Mieczysław drinks in small sips, Wilhelm Opitz lifts a finger (at that moment Wojnicz realises how essential that finger is to Wilhelm) and says in soft, slightly comical German:

“I wrote to Professor Sokołowski that you should stop over in Breslau. It is too long and too tiring a journey. I told him so.”

The broth spreads a pleasant warmth through Wojnicz’s body, and the poor fellow does not even know when he falls asleep. We keep him company a little longer, listening to his calm breathing, pleased that his lungs have quieted.

Our attention is now drawn to a streak of light thin as a blade's edge, which falls into the room from the corridor and comes to rest on the porcelain chamber pot beneath the bed. The cracks between the floorboards draw us in, and there we disappear.

A quarter to seven, the sound of a trumpet woke Wojnicz, and for a long moment he could not make out where he was. The tune was being played unmistakably out of tune, which amused him and put him in good spirits. It seemed familiar, but in the way of things so simple they verge on genius. It seems they have always existed and always will.

Mieczysław Wojnicz consisted of afflictions, and the person who knew them best was not even he himself but his father, January Wojnicz, a retired civil servant and landowner. He managed those afflictions with great expertise, gravity, and tact, treating the property entrusted to his care in the form of a son with immense responsibility and, naturally, love, though stripped of all sentimentality and all those "womanish feelings" he so detested.

One of those afflictions, in whose formation he had played some part, was his son's excessive fear that he might be being watched. Thus young Wojnicz devoted a great deal of attention to the gaze of others, checking whether it might be following him from behind a corner, from some nook, through a window where the curtain had folded back, through a keyhole. The father's caution and suspiciousness had turned into the son's obsession. He had the sense that another person's gaze was something sticky and clung to him like the soft, loathsome oral opening of a leech. That was why, in every room where he was to spend the night, he always carefully inspected the curtains at the windows, plugged the keyhole with a pellet of paper, checked for any holes in the wall, gaps between the floorboards, even looked behind the pictures. In guesthouses and hotels such spying is not, after all, so unreal; once, when he and his father were staying in a hotel in Warsaw during one of those health visits to a specialist, young Wojnicz discovered a neat round hole in the wall, clumsily disguised by the rich pattern of the wallpaper, and naturally plugged it with a pellet of bread, and when in the morning he tried to determine from where and by whom the guests might be observed, he discovered that behind the wall there was a service staircase used by the hotel staff. And yet! So he was not obsessed. People spy on one another. They love it, looking at a man when he is unaware. Judging, comparing. A man who is being watched becomes defenceless, becomes an unsuspecting, powerless victim.

After waking he immediately set about writing a message to his father, to set his mind at ease. It was only a matter of a few simple words, and yet it did not go well; his hand was numb and weak. So he focused all his attention on his hand as it guided the pencil point over the cream-coloured paper of a leather-bound notebook. This movement fascinates us; we like it. It recalls the same winding lines and spiral ornaments that earthworms tunnel underground and that the bark beetle gnaws into the trunk of a tree. Wojnicz was sitting up in bed, propped against two enormous pillows. In front of him he had a clever contrivance, a kind of table without legs. Its underside was a cushion stuffed with peas, so that it settled easily on the writer's knees.

First came two digits, forming "13", then a straight line and a cross, "IX", and after it another four digits making up "1913". Next, out of the flourishes emerged the word "Görbersdorf", gone over several times to make it bolder. The umlaut was treated with particular reverence. Then the pencil moved evenly and steadily across the paper. The graphite rasped, the paper yielded beneath the rounded shapes of the letters.

The room was modest, but comfortably furnished. Two windows looked out onto the street and the little river in front of the house, though the view was obscured by crocheted thread curtains. Beneath one of the windows stood a round table and two comfortable upholstered chairs, somewhat worn already, almost armchairs, a pleasant little corner for reading, should anyone wish to. To the left of the door stood a bed with a beautifully carved wooden headboard, and beside it a wardrobe. The dressing table had been placed to the right of the door. The walls were covered in fabric wallpaper with broad pale-blue stripes, which made the room seem taller and more spacious than it really was. On the wall hung prints from exotic lands: a herd of hares and a herd of hyenas.

Mieczysław Wojnicz described his impressions of the journey briefly, in Polish, converting one thousand nine hundred feet into metres (it came to almost five hundred), and entered those figures on a sketch map showing his trip from Lwów all the way here. The brief comments concerned chiefly the meals he had eaten on the road. Next to the place marked "Wrocław/Breslau" he added: "Yellow pumpkin soup, then purée with cracklings, cabbage, and a cutlet exactly like our pork cutlet. For dessert, vanilla pudding with meringue and blackberry compote, very tasty." Underneath he wrote: "Paid: 5 marks." He had promised his father he would write him a few words every day, preferably about how he was feeling, but in truth he did not know what he felt, and so he preferred to send recipes or geographical information back to Lwów.

There came a soft knock at the door and before he had time to say "yes?" a leather boot slipped into the crack between door and jamb and gently pushed it open; after it appeared the black folds of a skirt, the lace of an apron, and a breakfast tray, which was quickly set down on the table. The boots, lace, and apron vanished as quickly as they had appeared, and the bewildered Wojnicz had only enough time to pull the rug up over himself and mumble some greeting and thanks. He was so hungry that food was all that interested him.

He would describe it in his notebook presently: hard-boiled eggs, two, in lovely faience egg cups, covered with little caps shaped like hens; slices of smoked sheep's cheese garnished with parsley; a ball of the yellowest butter served on a horseradish leaf; a bowl of fragrant lard with a little spreading knife; sliced radishes; a basket of rolls of various kinds, light and dark; apricot jam in a glass dish; a mug of thick cocoa and a little pot of coffee.

At the full stop the notebook snapped shut, and Wojnicz ate with relish everything on the tray and, fortified by the meal, got up. Throwing a plaid over his shoulders, he padded to his suitcase, from which he took a neatly folded set of underwear, and then set about his ablutions. And as he rubbed his face with the towel, which had soaked up the scent of conifers omnipresent in the guesthouse, the image of their house in the country came back before his eyes, vivid as life, and of the washing dried in winter in the attic, which Gliceria carried up there in buckets when it was pouring outside. Back came the image of the attic, always full of dust, and the view from its little windows known as bull's-eye windows: the fields and the small park, and the bitter scent of rotting stalks of tomatoes, maize, and runner beans on poles. And by the laws of some not entirely comprehensible synaesthesia this image was carried over into bodily sensation: the roughness of clothing, the stiffness of collars, the angularity of freshly ironed trousers and the pressure of a hard leather belt. And it was there, in the attic, whenever he could, whenever he was left alone and for a moment far from paternal discipline, that he would strip naked and wrap himself in a satin tablecloth edged with soft fringe, and feel how deliciously the fringe brushed his calves and thighs, and think how wonderful it would be if they, like the ancient Greeks, could go about in such tablecloth chitons. And now, recalling that satin toga, he dressed and was glad to feel at last strong and rested.

We are witnesses as clothes appear layer by layer on his slender body, until at last his figure, wholly unlike yesterday's, coughing and grey-faced, stands with a hand on the door handle, eyes closed, imagining how it would look in the eyes of someone who might be looking at it now. He looks well: a slim young man with fair hair and delicate features, in grey worsted trousers and a brown woollen jacket. A moment later he opens the door decisively.

No, we do not consider this an obsession, merely an innocent hypersensitivity at most. People ought to grow used to being watched.

Wojnicz went downstairs at around ten o'clock, for he had an appointment for examinations at the kurhaus.

Throughout the house, because of the small windows sparsely set in the walls, a half-light prevailed; this is typical of mountain architecture. Here stood an oval table covered with a thick patterned cloth, a sofa and several chairs, while against the wall there was a piano, its infrequent use attested by solitary finger marks on the gleaming lid and a sheaf of yellowing sheet music. A small shelf hanging beside it was full of books about the region, the local ski routes and monuments. In an enormous glass-fronted cabinet a beautiful porcelain service shone white, with cobalt-blue sentimental scenes of shepherds and sheep.

“Gemütlich,” Mieczysław whispered to himself, pleased to have remembered a certain German word he was particularly fond of. His own language lacked such a word. Cosy? Pleasant?

The words of Doctor Sokołowski kept returning to him as well, from the time when the doctor had first begun treating him and wrestling with his apathy: that one must make life appetizing. Yes, appetizing, Wojnicz thought, that was a better word than gemütlich, for it applied not only to a space but to everything else too: to someone’s voice, to the way they spoke, the way they settled into an armchair, tied a scarf at the neck, to the way the biscuits were arranged on a little plate. He drew a finger across the table, covered in soft olive-green plush, and only after a moment noticed with alarm the thin man in the armchair by the window, with his pronounced birdlike features and wire spectacles perched on a prominent nose. A cloud of cigarette smoke enveloped him. Wojnicz’s hand sprang back from the plush as if scalded and vanished, embarrassed, into the clasp of the other one. The man, just as disconcerted at having his solitude discovered, rose and introduced himself rather formally, in German, with a strange Silesian accent:

“Walter Frommer. From Breslau.”

Wojnicz slowly and distinctly pronounced his own first and last name, probably hoping the other man would remember it at once. They spoke for a while, and Frommer informed him that he came to Görbersdorf regularly for treatment and had been here, on and off, for three years now. Sometimes he went back to Breslau for a short while, but as soon as he did, his condition worsened again.

“You see, the city of Breslau lies by the water. In spring swarms of mosquitoes hang over the buildings, small but extraordinarily vicious, and people fall ill with rheumatism. In summer it is impossible to sit out in the garden, which is why government officials stay there only for a few short years and then return to better places. Breslau is a city people only pass through” - sadness entered his voice, as though he pitied the city. “It is because of all that water; it creeps in everywhere... I do not endure it well...” He began to cough. “You see, the very thought of it makes me cough.”

Wojnicz hastily turned his gaze toward the window, beyond which a merry party was just then passing, bursting into laughter every few moments. He thought those people were laughing in Polish, though he could not quite have explained the impression to himself. From that distance no words could be heard.

“Are you too preparing to move into the kurhaus?” he asked Frommer.

He thought the question would bring at least the faintest smile to the other man’s face, but Frommer took it seriously.

“God forbid,” he bristled. “There are too many people there. You can see nothing from there. You will find out nothing and learn nothing. Life in a crowd is worse than prison.”

Well then, Wojnicz had probably already formed an opinion about Walter Frommer: an oddball.

They were both, it seemed, equally shy, for they stood facing each other in awkward silence for a moment, each waiting for the other to utter some conventional phrase. It was their host, Wilhelm Opitz, who rescued them from this stalemate.

“I hope I’m not interrupting a lively conversation,” he said, and for a moment Wojnicz wondered whether Wilhelm was mocking them or really was that inattentive. But then he took him firmly by the arm and led him towards the exit.

“Forgive me, but I have to place the young man under Dr Semperweiß’s watchful eye. Our guest arrived here in a sorry state.”

Frommer muttered something indistinct, returned to his place by the window, and sat down in exactly the same position as before. As if he were employed there as a smoking piece of furniture.

“Dr Frommer is a little odd, but he’s a decent man. Like everyone in my guesthouse,” Wilhelm said in that dialect of his, which was becoming more and more pleasant to Wojnicz’s ear, when they stood on the steps outside the house. “The boy will take you to Dr Semperweiß. Mind him, he doesn’t like people from the East. He doesn’t like anyone at all. It’s a great pity there’s no one here like Dr Brehmer,” he added pensively when, a moment later, the two of them stopped by the little bridge.

Wojnicz witnessed the fog now forming strange bands and flowing upward like smoke.

“Perhaps you know Dr Sokołowski?” he asked.

Wilhelm’s face brightened and came alive.

“It goes without saying I knew him as a child. He was friends with my father, who worked for him. We all work for the kurhauses here. How is he getting on?”

Well, Wojnicz did not know exactly. He only knew that he worked at a clinic in Warsaw, that from time to time he lectured in Lwów. His father used to take him for consultations when Sokołowski was in that city. And it was thanks to him that he was here.

“Is he still so slender?” Willi asked.

Slender? No, not slender. Professor Sokołowski was a stocky, stout man. Wojnicz did not have to answer this surprising question, however, because Rajmund emerged just then from the streaks of fog, yesterday’s coachman, a gawky boy in his teens, whom Wilhelm greeted in a rather peculiar way: with a light smack on the head. The boy took it as a perfectly natural, friendly gesture.

Now the two of them were walking down along the stream towards the centre of the village; Rajmund was saying something eagerly, but in such a strange dialect that Wojnicz understood very little. He looked with interest at the handsome houses lining the road and the workmen repairing the electric traction line. Rajmund asked whether Mieczysław knew what electricity was.

Then they bowed to two elderly women in wide skirts sitting on a bench in front of one of the houses.

“Frau Weber and Frau Brecht,” Rajmund said with an ironic smile, and that, at least, Wojnicz understood.

A moment later the boy proudly pointed out Dr Brehmer’s sanatorium, the same building Wojnicz had seen the previous evening, though now it seemed even more imposing, especially since the fog had almost vanished and somewhere high above the valley the September sun was shining generously.

Rajmund vanished as soon as he had led Wojnicz to the right door in the broad corridor. Now Mieczysław was taken over by a nurse whose eyes were rimmed with red swelling. A brief, polite smile exposed for a moment her large, yellowed teeth, their colour matching the worn gilt of the watch on a chain pinned to her apron. Above the pocket her name had been embroidered: Sydonia Patek.

Wojnicz had to do his time in the waiting room outside the doctor’s consulting room; the doctor had not yet returned from his rounds. So his fingers reached for the illustrated magazines laid out for patients, but his eyes found no calm in them; they could not focus on the Fraktur. To his surprise, though, Wojnicz found a newspaper in Polish, and his gaze at once relaxed wonderfully on the words of his mother tongue:

In Prussian Silesia, a quarter of a mile from the Bohemian border, eleven miles south-west of Wrocław, in a long valley running from east to west between the Riesengebirge and the Adlergebirge, lies, in the Waldenburg district, on the little river Steina, the charming village of Görbersdorf, famous for several decades now as a mountain climatic station for chest patients.

Görbersdorf stands 570 metres above sea level, in the belt that medical science calls “free of consumption”. The mountains surrounding it reach 900 metres. They shelter the village and its sanatoriums from the winds, which arrive here already weakened; hence the air in Görbersdorf is still as it is in scarcely any other valley.

He read no further, but folded the pamphlet in half and slipped it into his pocket. Now his attention was caught by a glass display case, where there stood a human torso made of wood — without head, arms, or legs, with its chest and belly opened up, showing internal organs painted in various colours. Wojnicz stepped up to the wooden torso to take a closer look at the lungs. They were smooth and clean, polished, shining with varnish. They resembled the fleshy petals of some monstrous flower, or the fungi that grow on tree bark. How wonderfully they had fitted themselves to the size of the chest, how they had reconciled their airy nature with the cage of ribs. He studied them carefully, trying to peer beneath the pointed tip where they reached the other, curled organs in their various colours. And yet he was disappointed all the same; perhaps he had expected something new, something he did not yet know. The solution to the mystery. Why he is ill. And why others are not.

When he returned to his seat, a familiar anxiety came over him, that agitation which always brings on the same bodily reaction: sweating. He would have to undress and expose his body to a stranger’s gaze. And panic: how was he to hide his shameful condition from the doctor. What he would have to say so as not to touch on all those sensitive matters. How to slip away. He had rehearsed it so many times.

When the doctor burst into the waiting room, he did not so much as glance at him; he strode across it at a rapid pace, with only the skirts of his white coat streaming behind him. He motioned with his hand for the patient to rise. Mieczysław all but trotted after the doctor into a large consulting room with an enormous window, full of glass-fronted cabinets, assorted medical apparatus, and strange chairs. Wojnicz was somehow not surprised to see a gun leaning against the doctor's desk - a large one, not a hunting gun at all, more likely a Winchester, with a beautifully polished stock. Without turning round, the doctor told him to sit down, and in this way Wojnicz felt safely hidden behind the desk, as if in a trench.

He handed the doctor Professor Sokołowski's letter of recommendation, but the doctor only cast a glance at it, plainly far more interested in the body sitting before him. The young man felt uneasy, and the reason was the way the other man was looking at him. As though he did not see Mieczysław Wojnicz, a patient from far-off Lwów, but the body itself, something mechanical, an object. First, without the least embarrassment, he pulled down Wojnicz's lower eyelid, studying attentively the colour of the mucous membrane and the eyeball. Then his gaze moved from chin to temples; at last he ordered him to strip to the waist, looked critically at his chest, and began pressing the patient's nipples with a finger.

"Slightly enlarged, as are the lymph nodes," he said. "Has it always been like this?"

"For a few years," Wojnicz replied, abashed.

The doctor seized him by the chin and ran a finger over the two-day, patchy, feeble stubble. He meticulously felt the lymph nodes; then his bold fingers percussed his back, bringing out a dull sound from it, a booming as if from underground. He did this with great care, centimetre by centimetre, like a sapper searching for a hidden bomb. It all lasted half an hour or so, until at last the doctor sighed and told him to dress. Only then did he reach for the letter. Then he said, looking over the metal rims of his spectacles:

"Phthisis." It sounded as if he had whistled. "Tuberculosis, and today, fashionably, Morbus Koch. You know all that, young man, don't you?"

Wojnicz fastened the buttons of his shirt and nodded.

"Not very advanced, to be frank. Just something small, a little seed of something. 'Phthisis' means disintegration, you know?" He pronounced the word, "Zerfall," with evident pleasure, stressing the r. "But here we deal with disintegration."

"Yes, Dr Brehmer's method..." Wojnicz began, but the doctor rose impatiently and waved a raised hand.

“Oh yes. Brehmer observed that travelling to Italy with tuberculosis makes not the slightest sense. Only mountain air truly heals. Air like this. Do you see?” The doctor went over to the window and for a brief moment fell silent, lost in thought. “We are here in a cauldron,” he said, making circular, extravagantly broad movements with his arms, as though he wanted to force the nature of the phenomenon more vividly on his listener. “Below us there is a great underground lake, which makes it warmer here than elsewhere. This air is rich in oxygen, but there is no wind. Lung diseases and epidemics were never known among the local people, would you believe it? No one here ever had bad lungs. And on top of that, the altitude remains within the limits necessary for treating pulmonary diseases, because it does not speed the heart up too much, as happens in places lying above nine hundred metres above sea level. Fir forest grows here, saturating the air with ozone, and ozone plays a key role in renewing the blood and the whole organism. Breathing alone will arrest the process of decay in your young lungs. Every breath is healing; think of it that way. Imagine that with every breath pure light is flowing into your lungs.” The doctor looked at Mieczysław through the lenses of his spectacles, which magnified his dark eyes in an unsettling way. “And besides that, we have other attractions here. You must simply submit, surrender yourself to the regimen of treatment. Feel as if you were in the army.”

He went over to the window and, with a movement of his head, showed him the patients strolling in the park.

“These are your comrades-in-arms.”

Wojnicz suddenly realized that he was not going to like this doctor. Gentle, kindly Doctor Sokołowski came back to mind.





“That is clear to me, Doctor,” he replied, tugging at the cuffs of his shirt. “I’d just... like to know whether I have any chance.”

“Of course you have a chance. Otherwise you would never have come here, young man. You would not have dared come here without feeling that you had a chance. You would have gone on quietly being ill in the East. It’s flat there, isn’t it?”

Wojnicz was now to learn many interesting things about the brilliant Dr Brehmer, who had bought the village of Görbersdorf and the whole surrounding area, more than a hundred hectares of woods and land in all, in order to found this sanatorium. Long before, Brehmer had observed that autopsies and examinations of living tuberculosis patients always revealed a disproportion between the heart and the lungs: their lungs were relatively large, while the heart was small, with thin, slack, weak walls. Earlier, no one had paid any particular attention to this correlation, and no one had thought of connecting this disproportion between the organs of the chest with the etiology of tuberculosis. Yet it seemed obvious that a small, weak heart led to a slowing of blood circulation, and thus to chronic ischemia of the lungs and pulmonary epithelium. Tuberculosis followed from that. Besides, Brehmer had studied the geographical distribution of the disease, and this had only strengthened his conviction as to the etiology in question. Travellers' accounts showed that there were places and regions where tuberculosis did not occur: the higher mountains in every climatic zone, Iceland, the Faroe Islands, the steppes of Kirghizia.

On the one hand, what mattered here were the special characteristics of the high-mountain climate. Lower atmospheric pressure causes the body to respond with increased cardiac activity and a faster pulse, since in this way it defends itself against oxygen deprivation; that leads to accelerated metabolism and a rise in body temperature. Secondly, lifestyle and diet were important: plentiful food, above all fat, kumiss with its alcohol content, hard physical labour.

The quickened pulse and the quickened action of the heart cause an increase in the volume of the heart muscle and a strong musculature develops in that organ; among the inhabitants of the regions mentioned, hypertrophy of the heart is in fact common, that is, a phenomenon the opposite of that in tuberculosis patients.

– My dear young man – Dr Semperweiß concluded his lecture. – That is our whole prescription. In Central Europe the tuberculosis-free zone begins at an elevation of roughly four hundred and fifty metres. Added to that is the constant supervision of a doctor, who regulates the diet. Besides that, exercise in the fresh air. Nature itself cures us.

Dr Semperweiß pulled out a sheet of paper and wrote down the recommendations point by point, commenting on them aloud in a voice already slightly bored:

– At least six weeks, preferably several months. Walks tailored to the individual patient, and necessarily along routes with varying gradients; there must be benches at short intervals along the way, so you don't overexert yourself. Moderate cold-water treatment. That is what will help you. Medicines in moderation. In the event of severe coughing fits, I recommend holding back the cough as far as possible, and when it cannot be held back, sipping cold water or soda water with hot milk in small sips. If bleeding from the lungs should occur, God forbid, we apply ice packs to the heart and lungs, as well as morphine injections. In the case of severe attacks, with shortness of breath and weakness, we first administer a strong stimulant, champagne for instance. Yes, don't be afraid of champagne, or of alcohol in general. But strictly in small amounts. Drunkenness is absolutely forbidden! In the event of fever, the temperature is first taken every two hours, to make sure there really is a fever. Night sweats are effectively treated by drinking milk in the evening with two or three teaspoons of cognac or a herbal liqueur. Sister Sydonia Patek will explain everything and show you how it's done.

As he delivered this whole speech, he was writing down the instructions, and Wojnicz marvelled that he could do both at once.

– You are staying with Herr Opitz, is that right? Every day you will come to the kurhaus for your treatments and your prescribed reclining, and as soon as a place opens up in the sanatorium, I shall let you know; here things are always shifting. Shifting, he emphasized. For the time being, Herr Opitz's guesthouse is just as good for your health as we are, or Doctor Römpler's sanatorium, and the daily short walks will bring a little colour back to your face.

The doctor sprang to his feet and handed Wojnicz the sheet of instructions. And that was that: he had been admitted.

Now he sat in the waiting room until the ugly nurse should prepare his treatment notebook and the other necessary papers for him. He took from his pocket the scrap of leaflet, folded into quarters, and finished reading:

It must be acknowledged, generally speaking, that from a therapeutic point of view the most effective cure so far is considered a stay in such places as Meran in Tyrol, Görbersdorf in Silesia or Davos in Switzerland, laid out on the model of Görbersdorf. Doctor Römpler's sanatorium, founded in 1875, lies almost directly at the foot of the mountains and consists of a suitable number of buildings in the form of elegant villas. A water main 1,140 metres long brings down from the mountain at whose foot the sanatorium lies crystal-clear mountain spring water, gushing straight from the porphyry rocks, into the elegantly appointed bathing rooms of the shower building.

Patients do not lack for occupations and amusements. The cure itself, meals and so forth occupy a large part of the day, and the charming countryside around Görbersdorf offers any number of excursions. The aim of the treatment is for the patient to strive to overcome his illness himself. He must be made resistant by strengthening the body. In this way the progress of the disease will first be arrested; then, little by little, the affliction will recede and health begin to return. Through constant gymnastics the affected lungs learn to function properly, while the fresh mountain air stimulates the workings of the heart. The results of the Görbersdorf cure may be counted among the most favourable. Almost 75 percent of patients recover.

It would be wonderful to believe that he belongs precisely to that seventy-five percent.




2.

Schwärmerei

Wojnicz was walking back to the guesthouse with a small notebook in which the history of his treatment was henceforth to be recorded, and turning over in his mind what Dr Semperweiß had told him. The most important thing in the cure is the regimen. One gets up early, very early in the morning. Temperature-taking. Noting it in the notebook. Before breakfast, which is eaten between seven and eight, gymnastics is obligatory; after breakfast, a walk, and on the way possibly baths by the Kneipp method and other treatments. Walks along a prescribed route. Second breakfast at ten: always fresh bread and butter and milk. Reclining on one of the many terraces. Dinner between half past twelve and half past one (soup with meat, a substantial meat dish with vegetables, then dessert and compote; on Sundays, instead of compote, something sweet is served, cake or a sweet flour dish). After dinner, coffee without fail in the winter garden or the pavilions. Then more reclining, then another walk, only the afternoon route must be different from the earlier one. Afternoon snack around four to four-thirty, and supper at seven: hot meat with potatoes, and with it, without fail, a glass of milk. In the evening the thermometer again, and a few sentences in the notebook about how one feels. Plenty of sleep. No excitement. Good, substantial food. Plenty of meat, milk, and sheep's cheese. Wojnicz decided to have dinner and breakfast at the kurhaus; he was to eat supper at the Guesthouse for Gentlemen. That was what he had been advised. When he moved into the kurhaus, he would take the rest of his meals there. Guests are summoned by a trumpet call.




He was bursting with excitement and resolve; it was that kind of euphoria that comes when one begins something new, something that irrevocably heralds a new beginning, when a person cuts himself off from the old and lets what has been slip into oblivion. Now even the severe, ironic Semperweiß seemed to him a herald of change.

As he walked, he tried to remember how the houses and guesthouses were laid out. He looked at the somewhat odd building of the astronomical observatory, where Dr Brehmer had supposedly studied the influence of the cosmos and the weather on the treatment of tuberculosis. Then he came to the massive edifice of Villa Rosa and turned back.

The full, golden September sun was shining. Mieczysław Wojnicz aimed each step at the middle of the large, flat stones with which the road was paved.

The two older women were still sitting on the bench in front of the house, silently shelling broad beans, snapping the dry, crackling pods. One of the beans suddenly sprang from one of their wrinkled hands and landed right by his shoe. Carefully he picked it up between two fingers and wanted to return it to its owners, but for some sudden reason unknown to him they leapt up from the bench and, taking their bowls and baskets, vanished into the house. Only their black, glossy skirts flashed in the sun. Well, nothing had happened. Wojnicz wiped the brown broad bean on his sleeve; it seemed perfect. He tossed it into the air and caught it. Not knowing what to do with it, he slipped it into his pocket.

He found the guesthouse door standing wide open, which surprised him, and then at once he saw a prayer book lying on the ground, flung straight into a puddle. The cream-coloured pages had already soaked through with dirty water. He picked up the prayer book and went inside, filled with sudden unease.

The drawing room downstairs was empty - presumably all the residents of the guesthouse were still away at their treatments. He set the little book, stained with mud, on the table and was just about to head upstairs when his attention was caught by the half-open dining-room door, and behind it shoes on the table, somehow familiar. Without thinking, as though hypnotized, he went up to the door and pushed it open to look at everything more closely.

The shoes were the lower part of an elongated bundle of indeterminate shape which finally turned out to be a human body. It lay in the dining room on the table where meals were eaten. It seemed to be well wrapped in swathes of cloth; to Wojnicz it looked as though it had on countless skirts, chemises, corsets, wraps. Wojnicz had never seen any woman from so close and so still; they always slipped past, they were always in motion. It was impossible to fix one's attention on them and make out every detail. But now such a body lay before him, and it was beyond doubt a dead body. He looked at the black lace-up ankle boots protruding from beneath the skirts and petticoats. The latter were finished with some kind of embroidery, but the lace was already somewhat worn from washing, its edges a little frayed. The bootlaces had been carefully tied in double bows; strange that someone who would be dead by afternoon had that very morning laced her boots so meticulously. The outermost skirt, made of a slightly lustrous fabric in thin black-and-grey stripes, fell neatly. Higher up there was something like a close-fitting jacket of dark, almost black cloth, fastened with round buttons of the sort Polish priests have on their cassocks. From beneath this garment a white shirt protruded, rather open at the neck, with a button torn off so that only the thread remained; the collar of this shirt had now been pulled high up under the chin, but carelessly enough that Mieczysław noticed on the neck a bluish-red weal, shocking against the white skin.

In the end he had to do it: look higher, at the face. To his horror he saw half-closed eyes, and beneath the lashes a thin glistening strip of eyeball. The twisted head was turned towards him, as though it wanted to confess something. On the lips, narrow and already somewhat bluish, he noticed the trace of a smile; it struck him as utterly out of place, as if ironic. The tips of the teeth protruded from beneath the upper lip, quite dry. And then there was something else: the face was covered with a fine pale down, like fluff.

He stood petrified, scarcely breathing.

In fact he recognized at once that this was the woman who had brought him breakfast that morning. At the time he had remembered only the boot that had pushed the door open. And the ample shapes constricted by a corset. Nothing more. Only here, after death, was it possible to see the woman as a whole.

“She hanged herself,” said Willi Opitz, stopping in the doorway.

Wojnicz started, frightened by the host’s low, resonant voice. Opitz announced it in such a tone as though he were stating some disgraceful negligence, something not to be tolerated. Yet his voice was trembling.

“Don’t upset yourself. The men from the mortuary will be here in a moment and take the body away. Rajmund ran to fetch them.”

Wojnicz did not know what to say. His tongue had dried to a sliver; he felt a band at his throat.

“When did it happen?” was all he asked.

“When? Just now, well, an hour ago. I went upstairs to her when she didn’t come down to collect the vegetables from the supplier. She was hanging. I cut her down. Go to your room, boy. Ah, here they are from the mortuary.”





“She brought me my breakfast this morning,” said Wojnicz, and despite himself the agitation could be heard in his voice. “That’s your maid, isn’t it?”

“Ah, no, no. That’s my wife.”

Opitz waved his hand as though shooing away a wasp and opened the door to the grim men from the mortuary, who began speaking to him in a hushed dialect. Mieczysław withdrew from the dining room; hurrying upstairs, he could hear their muffled voices, but he could not understand what they were saying. The whole conversation seemed to him like the low muttering of people who did not need words in order to understand one another.



Wojnicz sat down in the russet armchair adorned with a crocheted headrest. He was shaken. Strange, he had not thought that the kindly Willi Opitz had a wife. He should have known that men usually have some wife or other, who, not always plainly and distinctly visible, supports the family business from the kitchen or the laundry. Preoccupied with himself, his arrival, and his illness, he had not even noticed her. And now she was dead.
